assessment_items.question_content:
**Yinka Shonibare's sculptural installation is a recreation of  [[☃ dropdown 1]]**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bd9c9bea91d18b7ec15cf07c31958635.jpg)
hints:
Shonibare's *The Swing (After Fragonard)* is based on this painting:

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fd040057d72486111769f5d24dd1d2f5.jpg)

An example of 18th century French Rococo painting,  Jean-Honoré Fragonard’s *The Swing* depicts a coquettish young girl swinging in a lush and fertile forest and playfully kicking up her shoe.

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/global-culture/global-art-architecture/a/yinka-shonibare-the-swing-after-fragonard)**

choices.content: a 17th century Dutch still life
an 18th century French painting
a 19th century African sculpture
a motif common in Indonesian batik textiles

assessment_items.question_content:
**What does Shonibare commonly use in his work as a symbol of  complex histories of cultural appropriation, imperialism, and power?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Though clothed in the fashion of eighteenth-century French aristocratic style, Shinobare's protagonist wears a costume made of the ‘African’ Dutch wax fabrics that Shonibare has famously used to adorn his figural tableaux throughout his career.

Shinobare employes specific fabrics to symbolize the complicated histories of cultural appropriation, imperialism and power.  

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/global-culture/global-art-architecture/a/yinka-shonibare-the-swing-after-fragonard)**

choices.content: colorful textiles and costumes
metal guns and weapons
trees and natural landscape elements
found objects and everyday detritus
porcelain and imported ceramics

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which biographical detail informs Yinka Shinobare's artwork? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A British-born Nigerian, Yinka Shonibare grew up in both Africa and England.  His experience informs the interest in colonialism and issues of stereotypes and identity that he addresses in his artwork.  


**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/global-culture/global-art-architecture/a/yinka-shonibare-the-swing-after-fragonard)**

choices.content: his experience as a African man in England
his experience as an Indonesian man in Holland
his experience as a French man in Africa
his experience as an African man in Indonesia

assessment_items.question_content:
**All of the following statements about these colorful fabrics are true EXCEPT**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5b00d12c12118294bb98ade4fefd7f95.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The patterns on African Dutch Wax fabrics derive from Indonesian batiks and were manufactured in Holland and England in the nineteenth-century.  The Dutch marketed them to their West African colonies, where they have since been appropriated and integrated into local visual culture.

Although commonly associated with African identity, these colorful fabrics did not originate in West Africa.  

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/global-culture/global-art-architecture/a/yinka-shonibare-the-swing-after-fragonard)**

choices.content: the patterns derive from Indonesian batik textiles.
they were manufactured in the 19th century in both Holland and England.
they originated in West Africa.
they are often associated with African culture.
they highlight the link between economics and cultural identity.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which seems a likely interpretation of Shinobare's *The Swing (After Fragonard)* ? **

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bd9c9bea91d18b7ec15cf07c31958635.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Although Rococo painting emphasizes the leisurely, perhaps frivolous, lifestyle of the French aristocracy, Shinobare reminds us that it also reflects the political and ideological values of European imperialism in the 18th century.

All of these are possible interpretations of Shinobare's *The Swing (After Fragonard)* .  

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/global-culture/global-art-architecture/a/yinka-shonibare-the-swing-after-fragonard)**

choices.content: The leisurely lifestyle of the aristocracy was related to French imperialism.
Colonialist attitudes and racial stereotypes exist in contemporary society.
Identity is a product of intertwined economic, cultural, and political histories.
Rococo paintings demonstrate political and ideological values.
